articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 10
articles.content_text: Evolution Towards “Lighter, Smaller, and Longer-Lasting” Types of Secondary Batteries Secondary Batteries that Drove the Mobile Era After the appearance of lead-acid batteries about 160 years ago, secondary batteries have continuously evolved to be lighter, smaller, and longer-lasting.As a result, we now routinely use a wide range of IT devices such as smartphones, laptops, tablet PCs, smartwatches, cars, and drones, incorporating energy storage and usage seamlessly into our daily lives. Many of us have experienced anxiety when our smartphone battery drops below 50% and frantically searched for a way to charge it. This exemplif es how secondary batteries have shifted from supplementary providing emergency power to automobiles to a pivotal component driving today’s cutting-edge IT industry. Lead-Acid Batteries & Nickel-Cadmium Batteries Lead-acid batteries, primarily used as SLI Starting, Lighting, Ignition batteries in cars, electric forklifts, submarines, and golf carts, marked the beginning of secondary batteries. The demand for lead-acid batteries surged as the automotive industry gained momentum in the 1900s. While the car’s engine provided the primary source of propulsion, an additional power source was needed to start the engine and provide emergency lighting when the engine was off, and this role fell to leadacid batteries. These lead-acid batteries are commonly referred to as car “batteries.” Due to their simple structure and ease of mass production, lead-acid batteries expanded in tandem with the growing automotive industry. However, lead-acid batteries had notable limitations, including their heavy weight, large size, short lifespan, and rapid decline in performance if left discharged for too long, relegating them to a supplementary role. The secondary batteries began to see broader use in the late 1940s with the commercialization of nickel-cadmium or NiCad batteries. Nickel-cadmium batteries earned the nickname “all-purpose batteries” due to their small size, lightweight nature, and high power. Nevertheless, a signif cant drawback was that cadmium, a key component, posed environmental and health risks, and recharging the battery without fully discharging it could severely reduce its performance. 10 Part 1. Driving the Heart of the Battery Industry ㅣ The Principles of SecondaryBatteries

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 11
articles.content_text: Pb-acid 1859 Lead-Acid Batteries Ni-Cd 1899 Nickel-Cadmium Batteries Ni-MH 1990 Nickel-Metal Hydride Batteries Li-ion 1991 Lithium-lon Batteries Nickel-Metal Hydride Batteries & Lithium-Ion Batteries The next secondary batteries that followed nickel-cadmium batteries were nickel-metal hydride batteries that replaced cadmium with hydrogen. With enhanced energy density and stability, they found applications in new electronic devices such as mobile phones and laptops. However, they faced limitations in coping with the energy demands of fast-evolving IT devices. Ultimately, consumers sought secondary batteries with longer usage times and extended lifespans, leading to the emergence of an alternativelithium-ion batteries. Lithium-ion batteries can be intermittently and repeatedly charged without performance degradation. Furthermore, they are environmentally friendly as they do not employ environmentally harmful heavy metals like mercury and cadmium. Most notably, they provide a voltage three times higher than nickel-based batteries, making them well-suited for the latest IT devices requiring high capacity and power. Consequently, lithium-ion batteries are currently the most widely used secondary batteries across diverse felds, including laptops, tablet PCs, digital cameras, robots, drones, electric vehicles, and energy storage systems ESS Evolution of Secondary Batteries 11ENSOLPEDIA

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 12
articles.content_text: Lithium-ion batteries, which are composed of four elementscathode, anode, electrolyte, and separator, generate electricity through a chemical reaction in which lithium ions Li-ions move between the cathode and the anode. The battery is charged, when the lithium ions from the cathode move to the anode, and energy is released and discharged when the lithium ions from the anode return to the cathode. The operation principle can be easily understood by comparing it to our daily commute to and from home and the offce. The cathode is the space where lithium is contained and can be likened to a house where lithium ions reside. The cathode determines “capacity” and “voltage” in the performance of the battery, and the higher the proportion of lithium, the larger the battery capacity. Meanwhile the anode where lithium ions stay in a charged state is closely related to the lifespan of the battery. This is because graphite structure, a material used in the anode to store large amounts of lithium ions for a long time, is changed as it is repeatedly charged and discharged, reducing its ability to store lithium ions. Therefore, the development of next-generation anodes, such as silicon anode materials that can quickly store ions at a larger capacity instead of graphite, is also actively proceeding. The electrolyte can be seen as the transportation means for lithium ion’s commute. It serves as a medium that facilitates the smooth movement of lithium ions from the anode to cathode to produce electricity. An ideal electrolyte should possess high ionic conductivity, electrochemical stability, and a high ignition point. Additionally, it should allow the movement of electrons only through external circuits. Currently, liquid electrolytes are widely used as the best option to meet these conditions, but solid or gel-type electrolytes with greater stability and performance are also being developed. The last component of a lithium-ion battery is the separator. The separator prevents physical contact between the cathode and anode, where lithium ions reside, as if separating work and rest. Instead, the separator has very fine holes that allow the movement of lithium ions, and also automatically prevents movement when the temperature exceeds a certain level. Polyethylene PE and polypropylene PP are widely used as separator materials, and research is also undergone to make the separator thinner to reduce the size of the battery. Charging Discharging The Epitome of High Energy, High Power, and LongevityLithium-Ion Batteries Lithium-Ion Batteries 12 Part 1. Driving the Heart of the Battery Industry ㅣ The Principles of SecondaryBatteries

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 13
articles.content_text: Among the four components of lithium-ion batteries, the cathode material not only determines capacity and voltage but also signifcantly affects its stability and price. The cathode active material of a lithium-ion battery is primarily based on lithium cobalt oxide LCO and incorporates nickel and other metal elements. When composed of three elements, it is called a “ternary cathode battery.” Notable ternary cathode batteries include NCM and NCA. NCM stands for Nickel Ni , Cobalt Co , and Manganese Mn , while NCA represents Nickel, Cobalt, and Aluminum Al LCO, a prevalent cathode material for secondary batteries, is a compound of lithium, cobalt, and oxygen. The ternary cathode battery NCM, often called “NMC” in the West just a rearrangement of the initials , is more accurately termed “LNCMO.” This indicates that it is LCO combined with nickel and manganese. NCM, which is a combination of nickel, cobalt, and manganese, is primarily used as the cathode material in electric vehicle batteries. Its high power and enhanced battery capacity contribute to the extended driving range of electric cars. Nickel plays a pivotal role here. As the proportion of nickel increases, so does the energy density of the battery. Cobalt acts to prevent cathode corrosion and enhance stability. Produced as a byproduct from nickel or copper mining, it is a scarce metal, with over half of its global production originating from the Democratic Republic of Congo in Africa. This leads to signifcant price volatility and supply instability. Due to its importance, cobalt is referred to as “white petroleum.” The battery industry is developing high-nickel batteries to meet growing market demand for high energy density, increasing nickel content while reducing cobalt usage. “High-nickel” typically refers to batteries with a nickel ratio exceeding 80%. Lastly, manganese, alongside cobalt, improves battery safety. 3 elements Ni, Co, and Mn combined with LCO Cathode Materials Determining Battery Capacity and Voltage ➀ Ternary Cathode Battery, NCM 13ENSOLPEDIA

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 14
articles.content_text: 3 elements Ni, Co, and Al combined with LCO Alongside the NCM Nickel·Cobalt·Manganese cathode, the NCA Nickel·Cobalt·Aluminum cathode, another representative of the ternary cathode battery, uses aluminum Al instead of manganese found in NCM. A defining feature of the NCA cathode is its composition, which varies the ratio of nickel, cobalt, and aluminum according to the specific requirement of each battery application. Like NCM cathode materials that are composed of nickel, cobalt, and manganese, NCA achieves higher energy density and capacity by increasing the nickel content. The aluminum used in place of manganese not only enhances stability but also improves battery power output. Additionally, as the nickel content increases, the proportion of costly cobalt decreases, lowering production costs a characteristic shared with NCM. However, the higher energy density from nickel does come with the drawback of instability. For this reason, NCA cathodes are primarily employed in small battery markets, like cylindrical batteries, mainly for power tool applications. Cathode Materials Determining Battery Capacity and Voltage ➁ Ternary Cathode Battery, NCA 14 Part 1. Driving the Heart of the Battery Industry ㅣ The Principles of SecondaryBatteries

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 15
articles.content_text: 4 elements Ni, Co, Mn, and Al combined with LCO Given the critical role of the cathode as a core material in lithium-ion batteries, the battery industry has continuously worked on researching and developing new cathodes that encompass performance, stability, and cost-competitiveness. As a result, the “NCMA cathode” has recently gained attention. Composed of four elements and often referred to as a “quaternary cathode battery,” the NCMA cathode combines aluminum Al with the existing nickel Ni , cobalt Co , and manganese Mn of the NCM cathode. Essentially, it is a material that combines the high energy density of NCM with the superior stability of NCA. The NCMA cathode, a high-nickel cathode with increased nickel content and proportionally reduced more expensive cobalt, reduces manufacturing costs. Moreover, the significant content of nickel boosts the battery’s capacity and energy density. Additionally, the NCMA cathode can be used across all battery formats, including cylindrical, prismatic, or pouch, highlighting its wide use. These features allowed the NCMA cathode to improve EVs’ driving range, power, and battery safety, emerging as a key battery material for high-performance EVs. For reference, EV battery cells have distinct characteristics based on their shapes. Cylindrical batteries are similar to the metal cylindrical batteries such as AA or AAA consumer batteries commonly used in daily life. Their sturdy exterior and standardized size make them cost-effective and readily available. Prismatic batteries are encased in an aluminum can, which protects them from external impacts. Lastly, pouch batteries contain a battery within an aluminum-laminated flm pouch. They boast high energy density, leading to longer driving ranges. Their flexible exteriors allow for diverse sizes and shapes, and they are compact and lightweight. Cathode Materials Determining Battery Capacity and Voltage ➂ Quaternary Cathode Battery, NCMA 15ENSOLPEDIA

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 16
articles.content_text: A precursor is a substance that is one step away from becoming another material through a chemical reaction. In cathode production, the compound made from elements like nickel, cobalt, and manganese, before adding lithium, is the precursor for the cathode. The most used method for producing precursors is co-precipitation, a process in which a substance carries down other substances during precipitation. For example, when making an NCM precursor, the first step is to dissolve nickel, cobalt, and manganese salts to make a metal solution. Then, by mixing and stirring the complexing agent and pH adjuster, reaction and focculation occur, inducing precipitation. After washing and drying the precipitated substances, a cathode material is formed. Even if they are produced for the same cathode material, precursors can have different compositions. For example, precursors for NCA cathodes can be used with both, one containing nickel, cobalt, and aluminum and another composed only of nickel and cobalt. Furthermore, even when using a precursor containing the same nickel and cobalt components, the method for making the cathode can vary depending on the processing of aluminum. Precursors can be classified into secondary and primary particles depending on their size. Secondary particles are the larger ones with a size of 10-20 ㎛, and primary particles are those below 5 ㎛. As the demand for high-energy and high-power batteries grows, primary particles are becoming more important. Precursors with smaller particles can store more energy and prompt faster electrochemical reactions since they have larger contact surface areas. Normally, they are used together, with smaller primary particles, to fll up the spaces created between the larger and cheaper secondary particles to improve density and stability. With the growing electric vehicle market, Korean battery companies seek to reduce their reliance on imported precursors and to ensure a stable supply, given that precursors take up about 70% of the cathode material costs. Developing precursors with increased energy density and safety to prevent safety risks is crucial for these companies to take the lead in the global battery market. The Foundation and Core Compound for Cathode Production Precursor 16 Part 1. Driving the Heart of the Battery Industry ㅣ The Principles of SecondaryBatteries

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 17
articles.content_text: When a lithium-ion battery is charged, lithium ions are stored in the battery’s anode. These ions move to the anode for insertion during charging and move through the electrolyte to the cathode to release energy during discharging. During this process, the anode material determines the battery’s charging speed and lifespan. In other words, a long-lasting battery’s anode material can repeatedly insert and release lithium ions, and a fast charging speed means the movement of lithium ions is rapid. “Graphite” is the most commonly used material in anode materials. Graphite, consisting purely of carbon, is structured as thin layers with a repeating hexagonal shape, resembling stacked honeycombs. This robust structure allows for the stable insertion of lithium ions between the layers. Both natural and synthetic graphites are employed as anodes. Natural graphite boasts a larger storage capacity for lithium ions and is more cost-effective. However, upon lithium ion insertion, there’s a slight expansion in the graphite particle’s internal structure, leading to decreased effciency and a shorter battery lifespan. On the other hand, synthetic graphite, manufactured by applying high temperature, produces a stable internal and external structure, exhibiting minimal expansion and superior longevity compared to natural graphite. With the increasing demand in the electric vehicle battery market and the increasing need for nextgeneration anode materials, “silicon” is also gaining attention. If silicon is used as an anode material instead of graphite, the battery storage capacity per gram of anode increases by about ten times, making the design for rapid battery charging easier. Consequently, LG Energy Solution has put signifcant effort into developing silicon anode materials and, in 2019, it became the frst in the world to employ an anode material containing about 5% silicon in battery electric vehicle BEV batteries. Further, LG Energy Solution is currently developing technology to utilize anodes with a 7% silicon content. The Lithium-Ion Storage Space that Determines Battery Life and Charging Speed Anode 17ENSOLPEDIA

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 18
articles.content_text: Electrolyte Lithium Salts Solvent Additives Small batteries for electronic devices LiPF6 EV batteries LiDFOP LiBOB LiPO2F2 LiFSI + + + + LiPF6 In lithium-ion batteries, where the operation is based on the chemical reaction of lithium ions moving between the cathode and the anode, the electrolyte acts as a dedicated highway just for lithium ions. Just as cars reach their destination faster and safer on a wide, straight road, an eff cient electrolyte is essential for allowing more lithium ions to move between the cathode and anode quickly and safely. There are three key conditions for an effective electrolyte. First, it must have a high “ionic conductivity” to transport a large number of ions in a short time. Second, it must be chemically stable to prevent unintended reactions side reactions during battery operation. Lastly, it should have low freezing and high ignition points to function well in cold and hot conditions. The Exclusive Highway for Lithium Ions Electrolyte The Three Major Components of the Electrolyte Electrolytes consist of lithium salt, solvent, and additives. Lithium salts and solvents are responsible for transporting lithium ions between the cathode and anode, while additives provide functionality to the electrolyte. It should be noted that because electrolytes are mostly made by dissolving lithium salt in a solvent, they are commonly referred to as “electrolytic solutions.” One type of lithium salt, LiPF6, is the most commonly used due to its excellent ionic conductivity, solubility, and chemical stability. Some lithium salts like LiFSl, which exhibit high lithium-ion mobility at low temperatures, are also used to improve low-temperature performance. The solvent, a key component affecting the electrolyte’s ionic conductivity, should have high permittivity and low viscosity. Permittivity refers to the degree to which an ionic compound lithium salt is dissociated, and a higher permittivity leads to better dissociation of lithium salts, increasing the number of lithium ions in the electrolyte. Meanwhile a lower viscosity allows for faster movement of lithium ions. However, typical solvents have a trade-offif the permittivity is high, so is the viscosity, and vice versa. Therefore, optimal ionic conductivity is achieved by mixing cyclic carbonates with high permittivity and viscosity and linear carbonates with low values for both. 18 Part 1. Driving the Heart of the Battery Industry ㅣ The Principles of SecondaryBatteries

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 19
articles.content_text: + Solid electrolyte Anode active material Cathode active material Anode active material Cathode active material Binder Conductive additives Binder Conductive additives + Liquid electrolyte The last component of the electrolyte, additives, usually constitute less than 5% of the mixture but play a vital role in enhancing battery durability and performance. The appropriate amount of organic additives, such as VC Vinylene Carbonate , FEC Fluoroethylene Carbonate , and SN Succinonitrile , as well as inorganic additives like LiPO2F2 and LiBOB, are used according to the required performance. VC and FEC form a protective film on the anode, enhancing the battery’s lifespan. SN suppresses the leaching of transition metals generated as byproducts at the cathode as a side reaction, improving durability and suppressing gas generation. Inorganic additives like LiPO2F2 and LiBOB mainly act on the cathode, suppressing the generation of gases within the battery and reducing resistance. Next-Generation ElectrolyteSolid Electrolyte Research into “solid electrolytes” is also actively underway. Traditionally, a separator is placed in a battery with liquid electrolytes to prevent direct contact between the cathode and anode and improve internal stability. In contrast, solid electrolytes can serve as their own separator, removing the risks of side reactions or leaks due to temperature fuctuations or external impacts. They also allow the addition of materials that increase energy density, enabling the production of high-density batteries. Studies show that all-solid-state batteries can double the driving range compared to conventional electrolyte batteries of the same size. The ions in solid electrolytes do not fow like in a liquid but move through the solid lattice, maximizing electrode-electrolyte contact and minimizing resistance at the interface, thus enhancing ion conductivity. There are 3 types of solid electrolytes, each with its own characteristicssulfide, oxide, and polymer. Sulfde electrolytes have high lithium-ionic conductivity as they can form a broad interface between the electrode and electrolyte. Oxide electrolytes have lower ionic conductivity than sulfde electrolytes but have high electrochemical stability. Lastly, polymer electrolytes are versatile and have price competitiveness since they share similar properties and manufacturing processes with liquid electrolytes. Building on these materials’ properties, LG Energy Solution announced in 2021 its technological breakthrough in developing long-lasting all-solid-state batteries that operate well at room temperature. It also unveiled its plan to commercialize polymer-based all-solid-state batteries by 2026 and sulfde-based all-solid-state batteries by 2030. Separator 19ENSOLPEDIA

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 20
articles.content_text: Separator Lithium-ion batteries produce electricity as lithium ions move between the cathode and the anode. If the cathode and anode electrodes directly contact each other, a short circuit can occur, leading to safety hazards. The Lithium-ion Battery Separator LiBS does double duty as both a “wall” and a “pathway”, allowing lithium ions to pass through while keeping the cathode and the anode from touching each other. Therefore, the separator must have “high thermal stability and electrical insulation properties”, “uniformly sized pores”, and be both “thin and strong”. High thermal stability and electrical insulation properties are critical requirements for preventing safety accidents. Direct contact between the cathode and the anode can generate heat, increasing the fre risk. Additionally, if the battery’s temperature rises beyond a certain threshold, the separator self-closes its “path”, blocking lithium ion movement to prevent an internal short circuit. The separator must also possess excellent insulation properties. The pores in the separator should be consistent in size and large in number. These tiny holes pores allow lithium ions to move through. The more uniform and numerous these pores are, the smoother the lithium ions can fow. Lastly, the separator must be thin and strong. The thinner the separator, the more material can be added to the anode and cathode, improving battery energy density. Moreover, the separator should possess suffcient physical strength to withstand external physical shock to be able to perform its role. Its like having a shield that can block, for example, a spear that can penetrate anything, fulflling two seemingly incompatible requirements. Therefore, only polymer materials such as Polyethylene PE and Polypropylene PP are suitable for use as separators. Separators are generally classified as “dry separators” or “wet separators” depending on how the pores are created in these two materials. Dry separators create pores by pulling and stretching PE or PP membranes in one direction. The process is simple and suitable for mass production. However, keeping the pore size consistent and the membrane thin is not easy. As a result, dry separators are applied to areas that do not require high energy density, such as Energy Storage Systems ESS and electric buses. On the other hand, wet separators are made fat by extruding a mixture of PE, PP, and paraffn oil at a high temperature. The pores appear when the extruded PE and PP sheet slowly hardens and the oil filling the gaps is extracted. Although the manufacturing process is complicated and has high unit costs due to the use of more materials, wet separators boast high energy density thanks to their thinness. As a result, wet separators are widely used in small battery applications such as mobile phones and laptops, and high-energy and high-power battery applications such as electric vehicles. Ensuring Battery Safety Battery Safety GuardianSeparator 20 Part 1. Driving the Heart of the Battery Industry ㅣ The Principles of SecondaryBatteries

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 21
articles.content_text: Most commonly used secondary batteries in various felds like IT devices and electric vehicles are composed of elements such as nickel, cobalt, manganese, and aluminum, forming ternary cathode batteries NCM, NCA or quaternary cathode batteries NCMA However, with the surging costs of raw materials such as cobalt and nickel, the LFP Lithium Ferro Phosphate battery, which is cheaper to manufacture, is garnering renewed attention. LFP batteries use lithium ferro phosphate LiFePO4 as their cathode material, replacing cobalt with ferric phosphate. Iron Fe is one of the most abundantly available metals and is significantly cheaper and more environmentally friendly than cobalt Co Lithium ferro phosphate is highly “structure”, in which cube-shaped crystals are closely knitted in a lattice shape. As a result, the LFP battery is less prone to fre risks from overcharging or over-discharging, and it also exhibits less overheating, contributing to a longer lifespan. However, the LFP battery is deemed to be inferior to the ternary cathod battery in performance. This is because the LFP battery is heavier and has lower energy density, which is a disadvantage for electric vehicles in terms of driving range. In response, the battery industry is continuously researching ways to overcome the energy density limitations of LFP batteries, presenting various predictions and opinions about whether LFP or ternary/quaternary cathode batteries will dominate the market. Additionally, some even say the two players would coexist in the market, taking advantage of their contrasting strengths and weaknesses and serving different needs. Gaining Attention for Cost Competitiveness and Stability LFP Batteries Cathode material composed of LFP LiFePO4 with the addition of iron phosphate Can be manufactured at a low cost without using cobalt and nickel 21ENSOLPEDIA

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 22
articles.content_text: Conductive additives Active material Binder Conductive additives are materials responsible for the efficient movement of electrons between active materials and they play an important role in enhancing battery performance in both cathodes and anodes. Particularly in the battery manufacturing process, the most crucial phase is the electrode process, wherein the first step, active materials, conductive additives, and binders are mixed together to create cathode and anode slurries, and then, through coating and drying, these are formed into the f nal electrodes. As the era of electric vehicles accelerates and the competition for battery technology intensif es, the conductive additive that the battery industry is recently focusing on is CNT Carbon Nanotube CNTs, which are graphene layers arranged in a hexagonal mesh shape with six carbon atoms and rolled into a tubular shape, are long, linear conductive additives. Despite having a diameter of only one hundred-thousandth of a human hair, their strength is about 100 times greater than steel, and their electrical conductivity is comparable to copper. Adding these CNT to the cathode material can reduce the amount of conductive additives to onef fth of the previous level and allow the addition of more active materials, thus enhancing the energy density. Additionally, their electrical conductivity is superior to traditional carbon black additives, so adding only 20% of the amount can achieve similar performance, enhancing price competitiveness. Meanwhile using long linear CNT conductive additives in anode materials can have different effects. When added to silicon-containing anode materials, they maintain a robust conductive network despite signif cant expansion and contraction of the silicon active materials, greatly improving cell life. They also allow for a signif cant reduction in electrode thickness due to the high capacity of silicon active materials compared to traditional graphite, which is advantageous for rapid charging. These improvement effects contribute to the expansion of the CNT conductive additives market, which the battery industry expects to grow signif cantly from KRW 464.5 billion in 2021 to KRW 2.3917 trillion in 2027. Effective for Both Cathode and Anode! CNT Conductive Additives Structure of CNT 22 Part 1. Driving the Heart of the Battery Industry ㅣ The Principles of SecondaryBatteries

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 23
articles.content_text: The industry, academia, and research institutes are currently engaged in developing various materials for lithium-ion batteries that meet requirements for larger capacity, longer lifespan, and lower price. Among these, the area most focused on is developing high-nickel content in cathodes, which accounts for about 40% of the total battery cost and is crucial in determining the battery capacity, lifespan, and stability. This focus stems from the fact that nickel is a key component of the cathode active material that significantly influences the battery cell’s energy density. Enhancing the energy density hinges on increasing the capacity of the cathode active material, a role directly fulfilled by nickel. Higher nickel content results in increased energy density, extending the driving range per charge. However, increased nickel content also leads to significant drops in cycling stability and thermal stability, which is an issue that needs to be resolved first in developing high-nickel cathode materials. The rapid performance degradation in high-nickel cathode materials is mainly due to the instability of Ni4+ ions inducing side reactions with the electrolyte. High-nickel cathode materials are prone to microcracks during charging, leading to rapid capacity fading upon repeated charging and discharging. As the nickel content increases, the severity of these microcracks also escalates, necessitating the development of strategies to limit microcrack formation and reduce surface reactivity. In addition to the focus on high-nickel content, another emerging issue is the cobalt-free development of cathode materials. Cobalt plays a role in maintaining the structural stability of the cathode during chargedischarge cycles and enhancing rate performance1. In other words, it is an essential material for controlling corrosion and safety hazard risks in cathodes. However, Yang-Kook Sun Professor, Department of Energy Engineering at Hanyang University cobalt is a rare and limited abundance material, and its price has skyrocketed with the recent surge in battery demand. Amid the accelerating transition from internal combustion engines to electric vehicles, going cobalt-free has become necessary to ensure a stable supply of battery raw materials and to enhance price competitiveness. The current goal of cobalt-free development is to create Co-free cathode materials that do not compromise energy density, low-temperature performance, or rate performance. Various doping and coating strategies used in NCM and NCA cathodes can be employed to achieve this. However, traditional doping and coating techniques may not adequately address the fundamental issues. Hence, new techniques are being developed to improve the low rate performance of cobalt-free cathodes by adjusting the microstructure inside the cathode particles. The battery industry, which has signif cant growth potential, is particularly sensitive to changes in the market environment of the electric vehicle industry and the policies of various countries. It must meet rising and diversifying demands by developing technologies that encompass high energy, stability, functionality, and environmental friendliness. Since these characteristics are mostly determined by the cathode materials, the key component of lithiumion batteries, going high-nickel and cobalt-free will be even more important. 1. A capacity performance impacted by charging and discharging speed current A cathode with high rate performance exhibits high capacity even at rapid charging and discharging speeds. * This column was written based on the writer’s views and does not necessarily ref ect the opinion or strategy of LG Energy Solution. articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 24
articles.content_text: Insight Since beginning its R&D in 1996, LG Energ y Solut ion h a s c ont i nuou sly i mprove d a nd incorporated the four major battery componentscathode materials, anode materials, electrolytes, and separators in its battery production. This has led to a series of advancements spanning from lithium polymer batteries for camcorders and cylindrical batteries to batteries for electric vehicles. As a result of these endeavors, LG Energy Solution has achieved several world-first applications for materials in various areas. With growing interest in electric vehicles and battery performance, LG Energy Solution is continuing its research and development aimed at enhancing the performance of secondary batteries further, also focusing on cost competitiveness, rapid charging, and safety. To ensure sufficient driving range regardless of temperature or external environment, more increase in energy density is needed than what is currently available. Also, stable cost competitiveness is essential as prices frequently change depending on various factors such as policies, raw material price f uctuations, and technological advancements. Lastly, rapid charging and safety have recently become the most prominent development direction, requiring ongoing efforts to shorten the charging time and fundamentally prevent battery-related safety hazards. Based on this direction, the cathode material f eld is currently under active research, particularly focusing on NCMA single crystal cathode material using nickel, cobalt, manganese, and aluminum. Nickel plays an important role in increasing the capacity of batteries, while manganese enhances their safety. Additionally, aluminum adds to the stability of batteries and reduces the cost by lowering the expensive cobalt content. Other areas of active development include Mn-rich cathode material and Si-based anode material. Mn-r ich c at hode mater ia l u ses more t ha n 60% of the relatively inexpensive manganese, making it possible to deliver more reasonable prices. Thanks to its high stability and excellent performance, it is also planned to be used as the next-generation cathode material by improving its powder characteristics and developing necessary electrolytes. Si-based anode material can increase energy density more than four times compared to graphite-based anode materials. Research is ongoing to solve major problems like the expansion of silicon, during cycling using nanotechnology to signif cantly improve stability and battery life. How Far Have We Come? Current Development Status of the Four Major Battery Components Capacity Life Power Cost competitiveness Reliability NCMA It uses high-nickel content to increase the batterys capacity, manganese to enhance stability, and aluminum to boost both power and stability. By reducing the amount of cobalt, it can also lower manufacturing costs. This can be applied to all forms of batteries, whether they are cylindrical, prismatic, or pouch-type.24

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 25
articles.content_text: Dendrites formed on the anode Separator Dendrites Solid Electrolyte Insight Among the next-generation batteries that satisfy more energy, higher power, better stability, and lower cost, those attracting much attention include all-solid-state batteries, lithium-sulfur batteries, and lithium-metal batteries. All-solid-state batteries mean that all cell materials are solid, changing the electrolyte among the four main components of lithium-ion batteries cathode, anode, separator, and electrolyte into a solid. However, achieving more energy is diff cult since solids are heavier than liquids. Therefore, continuous research is being conducted, including changing the anode material to metal or making it anodeless, improving the ionic conductivity of solid electrolytes, and developing thin and strong solid electrolytes that can be manufactured successfully in high volume. Thus, LG Energy Solution’s vision for its nextgeneration all-solid-state batteries is as follows“No Anode”, where there is no anode active material; “No Separator”, operating with solid electrolyte f lm without a separator; “No Short”, suff ciently mobile and safe against internal and external deformations; “No Liquid”, eliminating the risk of leakage as there is no liquid; and “Non Flammable”, not catching f re even when directly exposed to f ames. Lithium-sulf ur batteries replace the nickel, manganese, and cobalt typically used in cathode materials with the non-metallic material “sulfur” as the cathode, and use lithium metal in the anode to obtain energy through the chemical reaction of sulfur. The lithium-sulfur battery, thanks to the lightweight “sulfur” cathode, is predicted to be the first next-generation, high energy density battery that will be mass-produced. Lastly, the next-generation battery that should be highlighted is the lithium-metal battery. This battery, which uses lithium metal instead of graphite as the anode material, was the prototype model for lithium-ion batteries that appeared in the 1970s. It was not used due to the “dendrite” phenomenon where lithium grows like branches, piercing the separator and causing a short by directly contacting the anode and cathode. However, with the recent development of new electrolytes and additives that can stabilize lithium metal, such batteries have rapidly gained prominence. In addition, world-wide active research is currently underway to develop lithium metal protective layers as well as separators that are diff cult for dendrites to penetrate. Continuously Researching and Advancing! The Next-Generation Batteries We Will Encounter To improve ionic conductivity, it is necessary to maximize the contact between the electrolyte and the electrode plates, and minimize resistance at the contact surface. The formation of dendrites on the electrode surface hinders the movement of lithium ions, reducing energy eff ciency. Additionally, if the dendrites grow, they can damage the “separator”, a core component of the battery. 25ENSOLPEDIA

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 28
articles.content_text: An Overview of the Battery Manufacturing Process All about the Lithium-Ion Battery Manufacturing Process The manufacturing process of lithium-ion batteries, which involves cutting-edge high technologies, consists of four main stepsthe “Electrode Process” for making the battery’s cathode and anode, the “Assembly Process” for fabricating the battery, the “Formation Process” for activating and stabilizing battery energy, and fnally, the “Pack Process” for modularizing the manufactured battery according to the target application. Step 1Electrode Manufacturing The electrode manufacturing process is a critical step that makes the cathode and the anode, which trigger the electrochemical reaction in lithium-ion batteries. It involves “mixing”, “coating”, “roll pressing”, and “slitting & notching”. The mixing process involves the mixing of the cathode or the anode active materials with solvents to create a slurry. Then, a “binder”, to improve adhesion between the particles of electrode active materials, and a “conductive additive”, responsible for facilitating electron movement among the active materials, are also added. Once the slurry is ready, the coating process applies the cathode slurry onto an aluminum foil and the anode slurry onto a copper foil to manufacture the electrodes. The electrodes are then calendared in the roll pressing process, and fnally, the slitting and notching process cuts the electrodes according to the designed battery specifcations, creating V-shaped notches and tabs for the cathode and the anode. ※※When manufacturing in cylindrical or prismatic shapes, the notching process is omitted. Step 2Cell Assembly The assembly process involves adding separators and electrolytes to the cathode and the anode plates created in the electrode process, thereby completing the battery’s packaging. This begins with removing any residual moisture from the plates by drying before stacking them. The stacking method, electrolyte injection, and sealing sequence differ depending on the battery’s shape and vary among manufacturers. For example, stacking electrode plates can be done in a “winding” method, which bends the plates into a roll, and in a “stacking” method, the plates are laid fat and folded like tissues in a tissue box. The winding method is used for cylindrical and prismatic batteries, while the stacking method is used for pouch-type batteries. After stacking, the electrodes and separators are combined to create a mono-cell and a bi-cell, preliminary products, followed by electrolyte injection. At this stage, both cylindrical and pouch-type batteries are injected with electrolyte and sealed. Part 2. Integrating Advanced Technology ㅣ The Manufacturing Process of Lithium-Ion Batteries28

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 29
articles.content_text: Step 3Formation Process The formation process involves activating electrical energy and ensuring stability, which includes repeated “Charging and Discharging” cycles followed by “Aging” or storage. During this process, pouch-type batteries go through a “degassing” step to remove gas formed during the initial charging due to electrolyte reactions. The batteries then go through a quality check. This step tests their performance, such as capacity and resistance, during the charging and discharging process. They are then stored for a certain period after which their OCV Open-Circuit Voltage is measured to identify low-voltage batteries. Finally, there’s an EOL End of Line process, where the batteries undergo performance verif cation and visual inspection. Step 4Pack Process To install the batteries in electric vehicles, they must be built into battery packs according to the requirements of the specif c car model. Therefore, after completing the formation process, the battery cells are modularized for the required application and then placed into packs during the pack process. This involves connecting multiple battery cells in a “Cell to Cell” format and f xing them in a module case. The attached cells are then connected, and a top cover is assembled to complete the module. Lastly, the modules are placed in a battery pack in a “Module to Module” fashion, completing the entire battery manufacturing process. articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 30
articles.content_text: Step 1 Mixing Coating The electrode process Slitting Roll-Pressing Notching Cathode Anode The electrode process is the most fundamental and key step in the entire battery manufacturing process and can be simply described as “the stage of making the cathode and anode”. The electrode manufacturing process is divided into several stages“Mixing” of raw materials, “Coating” the intermediary substance known as a slurry onto foil and drying it, “Roll Pressing” to densify of the electrode, and “Slitting” to cut the electrode to fit the size of the battery, followed by “Notching” to create electrode tabs. Making Battery Step 1. Electrode Manufacturing Process Making Cathode and Anode Mixing The f rst step in the electrode manufacturing process, “mixing”, is literally the process of mixing the raw materials. It involves weighing and thoroughly mixing cathode or anode active materials, binders, and conductive additives to create an intermediary substance called “slurry”. The equipment used for mixing is similar to the mixers used for kneading dough. The slurry produced in this mixer is then transported through pipes to a coater, where it is used for coating the electrodes. In the mixing process, it is crucial to uniformly mix the active materials, conductive additives, and binders in solvents such as NMP for cathode and water for anode in a short period of time. A binder is a material used to improve adhesion between active material particles and between the active material and foil. Normally, for the cathode, “PVDF Polyvinylidene Fluoride ” is used with a solvent called “NMP N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone ” that dissolves the binder. In addition, CMC Carboxy Methyl Cellulose and SBR Styrene-Butadiene Rubber -based binders are used with water for the anode. Binders not only assist in mixing but also help to maintain adhesion even when the electrode is in contact with the electrolyte or during an oxidation-reduction reaction, thereby ensuring battery life and performance. Hence, binders should possess high adhesion and good solubility, electrochemical and chemical stability properties. Conductive additives are used to ensure conductivity within the active material layer. If the amount of conductive additive is insuff cient or the mix of active material and Part 2. Integrating Advanced Technology ㅣ The Manufacturing Process of Lithium-Ion Batteries30

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 31
articles.content_text: Step 2 Coating Coating is a process in which the cathode and anode slurries, produced in the mixing stage, are applied and dried on aluminum and copper current collectors, respectively, at a consistent thickness and width. This is a crucial step in battery production, accounting for about 18% of the entire process and determining most of the variables in battery design. To coat electrodes reliably and uniformly without damaging aluminum and copper current collectors, advanced Roll-to-Roll equipment control, coating and drying technologies are necessary to remove the solvents contained in the slurry. Typically, electrode coating involves applying slurry to one side of the aluminum or copper foil, drying it at temperatures above 100°C, and then coating and drying the opposite side with the same thickness, pattern, and width. Among various coating methods, the “Slot Die” method is primarily used in the electrode coating processes. Slot die refers to a mold device frame that allows the slurry to pass through, enabling coating in a consistent pattern and thickness. In this sense, it is worth-noting here that LG Energy Solution has introduced the industry’s f rst “Double Layer Slot Die Coating DLD ” technology, which is an improvement on the conventional slot die, enabling the simultaneous coating of two types of electrode slurries onto current collectors. This technology is being used to stably produce automotive batteries and small batteries. DLD, compared to the existing single-layer coating technology, not only distributes the binder more uniformly on the electrode but also enhances the effectiveness of the binder. This platform technology improves charging rate, overall battery performance and productivity. conductive additive is not optimal, the active material layer may fail to achieve adequate conductivity, leading to increased resistance or reduced capacity in the battery. Therefore, it is essential to mix suff cient amounts of conductive additive, which is an electronic conducting substance, with the active material to reduce the resistance within the active material layer. Thus, in the mixing process, it is critical to evenly disperse the various materials added in optimal proportions, as this significantly impacts the performance of the battery. The mixing process in battery production is a key step that ensures good battery performance and quality by optimizing the dispersion state of active materials, conductive additives, and binders within the slurry. It is worth-noting here that in 2021, LG Energy Solution became the first company in the battery industry to apply 3,500-liter mixing equipment to mass production. Thus continuing its efforts towards securing industry-leading technology such as the mixing process to ensure superior battery quality and quality management. Active materials Conductive additives Mixer ENSOLPEDIA31

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 33
articles.content_text: Step 3 Step 4 Roll Pressing “Roll pressing”, also known as the “Calendaring” process, determines battery electrode density, performance, and surface quality. It involves passing the coated electrode through two rolling rolls to increase its density. The reason for performing the roll pressing process is that when large rolls press the electrode under hundreds of tons of load the electrode density increases. This, in turn, facilitates faster and more uniform movement of lithium ions among the active materials within the electrode, thereby enhancing battery performance. The structure of a rolling mill used in the roll pressing process consists of a “Working Roll” that applies pressure to the material and a “Back-up Roll” that adds pressure to the working roll. Typically, in the battery f eld, a relatively simple pair of rolls of the same diameter is commonly used. Recently, tandem rolling mills, whereby roll presses are conf gured consecutively, have been applied to increase the electrode density. During the roll pressing process, it is necessary to set the “rolling density g/cc ”, which determines the density of the electrodes. Rolling density refers to the density of the slurry, composed of active materials, conductive additives, and binders, after rolling. To achieve electrodes with higher density, more load must be applied to the electrodes during roll pressing. Generally, cathodes composed of nickel Ni , cobalt Co , and manganese Mn require a higher rolling load compared to anodes made of graphite. Slitting and Notching The electrodes that have gone through the roll pressing process need to undergo “slitting and notching”, which involves cutting them twice to fit the battery size. “Slitting” refers to cutting the electrode vertically, while notching is the process of making a V-shaped notch and tabs. During the slitting process, the blade changes according to the size of the battery cell being produced. The slitting process is followed by the notching process. The uncoated parts of the electrode, where the cathode and anode active materials are not applied, are called the “blank area”. Here, tabs necessary for lead welding are made, and unnecessary parts are removed. Notching can be done using either a press machine with blades or a laser machine, the latter being mostly used. This preference is due to the possibility of inconsistent cutting when the blades of press equipment become worn. Furthermore, cutting with laser equipment is advantageous for speeding up subsequent processes and for avoiding the hassle of changing molds, thus reducing maintenance costs. ENSOLPEDIA33

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 34
articles.content_text: Winding method Tab welding The assembly of the “cylindrical battery”, the most commonly encountered battery form in our daily life, starts with “winding”. Winding is a process where a machine called a winder rolls up the cathode and anode electrodes. This process has the advantage of being very fast, similar to how a “tissue roll” can quickly wrap the paper around the tissue core. During winding, cathode, anode, and separator rolls are unwound, and an aluminum tab is attached to the cathode’s blank part while a copper tab is attached to the anode’s blank part. Then they are attached to the winder in the order of cathodeseparator-anode-separator, wound around a mandrel, like rolling toilet paper around its core, and then cut into an appropriate size to form a jelly roll. Once the jelly roll is formed, it goes through various assembly processes to take the form of the familiar cylindrical battery. At f rst, the jelly roll produced by the winding method is inserted into a can so that the anode touches its bottom and the anode tab is bent at a 90-degree angle to make contact with the anode. After inserting the jelly roll into the can, it is attached using a resistance welding method. Resistance welding is also known as “spot welding” since bead-like spots are created when the metal melts and solidif es. After welding, a process called “beading” is used to create the neck of the cell, allowing the top cap to be f tted later. Once the cylindrical shape of the battery is completed through beading, electrolyte is injected. To do this, a vacuum is created inside of the can and the electrolyte is injected into it through a nozzle. Then, the can is pressed to let the electrolyte fill the pores of the electrode. Once the electrolyte injection is complete, the cathode tab and the top cap are attached to the cell using laser welding. Finally, the cylindrical battery is completed by mechanically sealing it through the crimping and sizing processes, producing it to standardized dimensions. After the assembly process is completed, the battery undergoes cleaning, quality verif cation, and formation processes before being delivered to the customers. Making Battery Step 2. Assembly Process A Cylindrical Batteries Part 2. Integrating Advanced Technology ㅣ The Manufacturing Process of Lithium-Ion Batteries34

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 36
articles.content_text: Making Battery Step 2. Assembly Process B Pouch-Type Batteries Lamination & Stacking To place battery materials into the electrode pocket of a pouch case, a mono-cell is required. A mono-cell is the basic unit of a battery, consisting of a cathode, an anode, and two separators arranged in a layered structure, such as “separator central electrode separator external electrode.” The lamination and stacking processes are applied to this mono-cell. To be more specific, the process begins with lamination, a method for aligning and bonding the cathode, anode, and separator. This is followed by stacking, during which monocells are repeatedly layered. Finally, stacking half-cells — each made up of two separators and an anode — forms a cell stack, where the cathode and anode are alternately layered with separators in between. Lamination & stacking technology maximizes space eff ciency within the pouch case, further enhancing energy density. Stacking, which involves individually layering battery cells, requires advanced technical expertise. By applying lamination & stacking, misalignment between cells is minimized, and empty spaces within the battery cell are eliminated, resulting in higher energy density. LG Energy Solution uses its proprietary lamination & stacking technology with mono-cells to produce a range of pouch-type batteries, from ultra-thin batteries under 2mm to long cells. Packaging The cell stack, produced through lamination and stacking, then moves to the packaging stage. First, leads are welded onto the cell stack, which comprises multiple layers of mono-cells. Next, a pouch f lm is compressed to form a pocket, and the lead-welded cell stack is inserted into this pouch pocket for assembly. To seal the pocket, heat and pressure are applied using a sealing tool. During this process, two pockets are formedthe electrode pocket and the gas pocket. The electrode pocket houses the cell stack, including the cathode, anode, separator, and electrolyte. The gas pocket, Meanwhile serves as an entry for electrolyte injection and as a space to store gases generated during the injection process. After injecting the electrolyte into the gas pocket, the injection port is sealed with the sealing tool, completing the assembly of the pouchtype battery. In the following formation process, any unwanted gases that are generated and accumulated in the gas pocket are removed during the degassing stage by trimming the gas pocket. Sealing Injecting the electrolyte Part 2. Integrating Advanced Technology ㅣ The Manufacturing Process of Lithium-Ion Batteries36

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 38
articles.content_text: Improving Electrode Alignment for Greater Stability Advanced Z-Stacking AZS AZSIntegrating Lamination & Stacking with Z-Stacking LG Energy Solution continues to develop manufacturing methods and integrate technologies to improve battery performance and safety. As part of this effort, the company introduced Advanced Z-Stacking AZS by incorporating the strengths of Lamination & Stacking L&S into conventional Z-Stacking to further enhance battery safety. As previously discussed, LG Energy Solution’s L&S method stacks mono-cells composed of electrodes and separators in layers, then combines them with half-cells consisting of separators and anodes, followed by taping to complete the structure. This approach enhances space utilization within the battery while increasing production efficiency. In contrast, Z-Stacking arranges cathodes, anodes, and separators in a zigzag pattern. The separator is folded in a Z-shape, and individually cut electrode sheets are sequentially inserted between the folds to form a single cell. LG Energy Solution’s proprietary manufacturing method, AZS, integrates this Z-Stacking structure with its lamination technology. A key feature is the addition of a Heat Press process to f nalize the stacked cell. The Heat Press process applies heat and pressure to secure the electrodes, ensuring precise positioning of the cathode and anode within the separator and improving electrode alignment and structural stability. LG Energy Solution’s Advanced Z-Stacking AZS Cathode Anode Heat and pressure Heat and pressure Separator Z-Stacking Lamination Heat Press Cell stack Part 2. Integrating Advanced Technology ㅣ The Manufacturing Process of Lithium-Ion Batteries38

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 39
articles.content_text: Optimizing Safety, Productivity, and Space Utilization The AZS method offers a higher level of safety compared to conventional Z-Stacking. The separator structurally isolates the cathode and anode, signif cantly reducing the risk of internal short circuits. In addition, the Heat Press process bonds the electrodes and separators, ensuring precise electrode alignment and preventing electrode displacement during operation. In AZS, adhesion between electrodes and separators is a critical factor. Insufficient adhesion can lead to lithium plating during charging and discharging, potentially causing capacity degradation or safety issues. By contrast, excessive adhesion can reduce the electrode surface area during thermal expansion and contraction, which may cause electrical short circuits. Therefore, precise control of adhesion is essential. LG Energy Solution’s AZS method strengthens adhesion between electrodes and separators through the Heat Press process, effectively preventing electrode movement during process transfer and fundamentally resolving the overhang1 issue, thereby improving safety signif cantly. Since cell stacks are pre-bonded, a larger number of cells can be handled during process transfer, reducing cycle time and increasing production eff ciency. In addition, equipment can be conf gured in unit-based modules, optimizing space utilization. Mass Production of Batteries Using AZS LG Energy Solution’s AZS method is currently in use in battery production. The Ochang Energy Plant has completed pilot-line validation and sample production using AZS. The technology has also been applied to mass production at PT HLI Green Power in Indonesia, a joint venture between LG Energy Solution and Hyundai Motor Group, starting in April 2024. Within four months of implementation, it achieved a yield exceeding 99%. Overhang Separator Separator Standard cell stack Cell stack with overhang 1. A condition in which some electrodes protrude beyond others in a stacked structure. ENSOLPEDIA39

articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 40
articles.content_text: Intercalation Electrolyte SEI Layer Anode While the previous assembly process shaped the batterys exterior, the formation process grants the battery its electrical energy characteristics, enabling it to function as a secondary battery and verifying its stability. The formation process involves large-scale automation for aging and repeated charging and discharging as well as storing the battery at a specif c temperature to assess its initial performance and quality. Depending on the model, batteries are initially stored for about 1.5 to 3 days during the first aging phase. To allow the electrolyte injected during the assembly process to inside the battery, facilitating smooth movement and uniform transport of lithium ions between the cathode, anode, and separator. After aging, batteries are partially charged and then they undergo a second aging phase at high temperatures of 6070°C. Meanwhile when the battery is charged for the f rst time, a thin layer known as the SEI Solid Electrolyte Interphase forms between the anode and the electrolyte. This solid layer blocks electrons while allowing lithium ions to pass, inf uencing the batterys stability, performance, and lifespan. The second aging process is carried out to ensure the formation of a stable and uniform thickness of the SEI layer. Once this process is complete, pouch-type batteries go through a “degassingprocess to remove gases formed due to side reactions of the electrolyte during the initial charging. Afterward, the batterys capacity and resistance are measured during the second charging and discharging phase. It is then recharged up to the required state of charge SoC specified by the customer and stored for about a week to measure the open circuit voltage OCV and sort out lowvoltage defects. Finally, at the end-of-line EOL , voltage, resistance, thickness, weight, insulation, and other factors are measured along with exterior inspection and packaging, concluding the formation process. Note that the sequence of these formation processes may vary among manufacturers. Making Battery Step 3. Formation Process Activating the Functionality of the Battery Part 2. articles.title: All About Battery ENSOLPEDIA - page 42
articles.content_text: Cell to Cell, Module to Module After completion of the formation and acquiring electrical characteristics, batteries are either delivered as individual cells or modularized to suit or ft electric vehicle models. The pack process involves modularizing the battery cells and placing them into packs before fnal delivery. The frst step in the pack process is to bundle battery cells to build a module. First, several surfacecleaned battery cells are arranged in a “cell to cell” confguration, which are retained into a module case with adhesives. Then bus bars and leads are welded to connect to the cells with a laser welder, and the module build is completed with installing module top cover. Finally, a pack build is completed when these modules are placed into the battery pack enclosures and connected each other. These complete battery packs are then installed in many applications such as electric vehicles and various IT devices. The number of cells and modules in a battery pack may vary, depending on the battery cells and application models. Making Battery Step 4. Pack Process Modularizing Battery Cells BMS and Cell Balancing Since a battery pack contains multiple cells, balancing and managing these cells is critical. Therefore, once a pack is assembled, the battery is monitored, and its performance maintained in optimal conditions using a control system called BMS Battery Management System , which estimates the batterys charging state and life. The CMS Cell Monitoring System inside the BMS measures the cellscondition and performs balancing to ensure optimal battery performance, safety and life. “Cell balancing” refers to adjusting the voltage differences between two or more serially connected cells. For instance, if two cells connected in series reach voltages of 4.2V and 4.0V, respectively, during charging, cell balancing adjusts this 0.2V difference to equalize the voltage. Voltage imbalance can occur due to manufacturing or external factors, and it can affect battery capacity and life, posing risks of overcharging, over-discharging, and fre hazards. Hence, cell balancing is essential. Since a single pack contains numerous cells, balancing and managing cells is very important. Part 2. Integrating Advanced Technology ㅣ The Manufacturing Process of Lithium-Ion Batteries42
